notes.note: The Kerameikos was a district of Athens (potters' quarter and cemetery) bordering the Academy. Cicero teases the Athens-resident Atticus by matching his fashionable Greek dateline with his own homely "I was at the Tusculan villa."

Composite herms: a square pillar topped by two divine heads back to back (here Hermes and Athena). The Latin actually reads Hermeraclae, herms of Hermes and Heracles; the rendering follows the better-known Hermathena Cicero prizes elsewhere (Att. 1.1, 1.4).

Typi: figured relief plaques set into wall-stucco as ornament. With the "figured well-heads" (putealia sigillata, decorated puteals capping cisterns) these are further Greek-style fittings for Cicero's villa.
Atticus's sister Pomponia, married to Cicero's brother Quintus; her pregnancy is offered as proof that the often-strained marriage is going well. The child is the younger Quintus.

Cicero was a candidate for the aedileship of 66 BC; aediles were elected by the comitia tributa. He releases Atticus from any duty to return to Rome to canvass, urging him to stay on his own business in Greece.
The recurring joke (cf. Att. 1.8): little Tullia "summons Atticus to court" over an unsent gift and names her father as the surety (sponsor) bound to make it good.
